title: 20 Second Parity Lifetime in an InAs--Pb Tetron Device

abstract: A central promise of topological quantum computing is that increasing the excitation gap improves device performance significantly. Here, we experimentally validate this principle in an InAs--Pb tetron device via interferometric single-shot parity measurements. By replacing aluminum with the higher-gap superconductor lead in our superconductor-semiconductor hybrid devices, we have improved the robustness of our topological phase. In addition, to enable fast and precise bring-up at scale, we have developed an rf measurement technique that resolves low-energy wire-end states and directly measures their energy splitting with $μ\text{eV}$ precision. We employ this technique to bring up a device in a multi-tetron array and perform parity measurements of one of the tetron's hybrid nanowires (NWs). By controllably switching the wire parity, we observe $h/2e$-periodic bimodal shifts in the quantum capacitance of a quantum dot coupled to the hybrid nanowire in an interference loop. Further time-resolved measurements reveal a characteristic parity switching time of $\sim 20$ s with some instances reaching minute-scale. Such extremely long parity lifetimes are orders of magnitude longer than typical qubit operation times, which are on the order of $μ\text{s}$. Finally, we discuss potential implications for the fidelity of Pauli measurements.
summary: This work experimentally validates the use of high-gap superconductors (Pb) in InAs tetron devices to enhance topological protection. By measuring the parity switching time, the authors report a lifetime of $\sim 20$ seconds, significantly exceeding current qubit timescales. This demonstrates that increasing the superconducting gap effectively suppresses quasiparticle poisoning, a key requirement for scalable topological quantum computation.
main_problem: To experimentally validate that increasing the superconducting excitation gap improves the robustness and lifetime of topological superconducting phases used for quantum computing.
main_result: The authors achieved an extremely long parity lifetime of $\sim 20$ seconds in an InAs--Pb tetron device, which is orders of magnitude longer than typical qubit operation times.
method: The study employs interferometric single-shot parity measurements and develops a novel RF measurement technique to probe low-energy wire-end states.
model_or_system: The physical platform is an InAs--Pb tetron device, a superconductor-semiconductor hybrid structure designed to host Majorana zero modes (MZMs) for topological quantum computation. The system's robustness is enhanced by using the higher-gap superconductor Lead (Pb) instead of Aluminum (Al).
key_observables: Parity switching time ($	au_Z$), quantum capacitance shifts, and the topological gap ($\Delta_T$).
important_parameters: Parity lifetime ($\sim 20$ s), Topological Gap ($\Delta_T \sim 70 \mu	ext{eV}$), Majorana hybridization splitting ($E_M < 1 \mu	ext{eV}$).
main_assumptions: The improvement in robustness is directly attributed to the increased superconducting gap, which suppresses quasiparticle poisoning effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the device architecture, material characterization (SdH oscillations, mobility), the RF spectroscopy setup, and time-resolved measurements showing the exponential decay of the parity state over seconds.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from demonstrating improved material quality and gap size using Pb, to developing high-precision RF spectroscopy to measure low-energy states, and finally culminates in time-resolved measurements to quantify the dramatically extended parity lifetime.
why_interesting: The demonstrated long parity lifetime directly addresses a major decoherence bottleneck in topological quantum computing, providing a crucial experimental benchmark for fault-tolerant quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scalable unit cell for multi-qubit tetron array. (a) Schematic of the tetron-based unit cell used for multi-qubit devices. Each tetron consists of two parallel proximitized NWs connected by a superconducting backbone, forming an H-shaped island that hosts four Majorana zero modes (MZMs) at the wire ends when tuned into the topological regime. Color- coded gates illustrate the three-layer architecture: electrostatic tuning gates set the nanowire density, junction and cutter gates control tunnel couplings to define and reconfigure interfero- metric loops, and gate-defined quantum dots (QDs) provide dispersive parity readout. Neighboring tetrons are coupled via shared QDs, enabling...

FIG. 2. Enhanced material stack. (a) Schematic of a hybrid material stack combining Pb with a composite quantum well. (b) Conductance as a function of bias voltage and split- gate voltage in a superconducting quantum point contact to a 2D Pb-covered half-plane in the tunneling regime. A large induced gap is clearly visible. (c) Shubnikov–de Haas oscillations, showing long quantum lifetime and large spin- orbit coupling in a shallow quantum well under Pb, as obtained via the analysis procedure of Ref. 78. We extract a density of 0.49±0.02×1012 cm−2, a spin-orbit coupling of 12±2 meV nm, and a quantum lifetime of 0.37 ± 0.14 ps. (d) Mobility as a function of density in a buried quantum well...

FIG. 3. (a) Cross-sectional TEM of a narrow Pb strip on the surface of semiconductor barrier and quantum well, dashed lines indicate interfaces at the top and bottom of the quantum well. (b) Measured localization length as a function of gate voltage extracted from 14 wires with different lengths of 1.25 µm and 3 µm. The estimated 1D subband onsets are extracted from quantized local conductance steps. (c) Phase diagram obtained from a TGP analysis of transport data from a 3 µm-long nanowire test structure. (d) B-field line cut along Vp = 3.2165 V showing local and anti-symmetrized non-local conductance along the dashed line in panel (c). Zero-bias peaks at the opposite ends of the NW persist...

FIG. 4. Wire spectroscopy. (a,b) Schematic of the quasi- particle injection procedure. The injector QD leads to a parity switch of the wire state, which, for finite EM, shifts the wire- readout QD resonance condition. (c,e) Mean of the normalized rf signal measured on QD1 (QD2) averaged over QD2 (QD1) configurations where S is the measured rf signal along the quadrature with maximal signal and σ the noise along the orthogonal quadrature. (d,f) Difference of the normalized signal measured in configurations (a) and (b). For better visibility, the insets in panels (c–f) each show a zoom-in on parts of a single QD-wire transition. (g) Extracted wire state energy EM based on analyzing the...

FIG. 5. Regions with a stable low-energy state at both ends of the wire detected by rf-based wire tuning. (a,b) Fraction of measured left (right) wire cutter configura- tions where the signal measured on the left (right) QD is above the selected threshold S/σ > 2.5 where S is the measured rf signal along the quadrature with maximal signal and σ the noise along the orthogonal quadrature. (c) Regions where the measured signal at both ends of the wire is above threshold for at least 7/10 cutter configurations.
